content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conducting an orchestra you can’t see. Photo by Jonas Baumann via Pexels.

6 min read
Just now

--

It’s been a while since my last post. Not for lack of material: the way I build software has changed more this past year than in the ten before it. I’ve just been too busy living inside that change to write about it. Time to catch up.
Everyone has a story about their AI workflow. I had one too. I would have told you I spend most of my time planning, that I keep things disciplined, that the agents do the typing while I do the thinking.
Stories are cheap. I wanted the data.
So I exported my full Claude Code history: 1,290 conversations, 488 MB of logs, around 38,000 of my own prompts across roughly 40 projects. Then I pointed Claude at the export and asked it to tell me how I actually work. Because if there’s one thing these AI models are really, really good at, it’s finding patterns.
Part of the answer confirmed what I believed. Part of it did not. It turns out I run the same loop almost every time, like a personal constitution I recite to the model. And the place where my time really goes is not where I thought.
The loop
Stripped of project specifics, this is the shape that shows up over and over:
ticket + spec
→ /grill-with-docs stress-test the spec before any code
→ implementation plan
→ /handoff dump the plan into a handoff file
→ /clear fresh session picks up the handoff
→ git worktree off main
→ subagents build it, each one using /tdd
→ small, stacked PRs
→ /pre-pr-review before opening each one
→ open PR, fix bot review comments
→ keep the stack rebased as main moves
→ close the loop: update the ticketEvery arrow in that diagram appears dozens of times in my prompts, often phrased almost word for word. The rules I repeat most:
- “Keep PRs small, reviewable and testable.”
- “I want pull requests always stacked on top of each other.”
- “Use subagents and use /tdd within those agents.”
- “Run /pre-pr-review before opening a PR.”
Four parts of this loop turned out to be the most distinctive, so let me walk through them.
1. I never go straight to code
The first thing I do with a meaty ticket is run a grilling session: the model relentlessly interrogates the spec against the domain before a single line of code exists. Only after that do I ask for an implementation plan, and only after the plan do I let anything get built.
Not every feature deserves the full interrogation, though. For simpler ones I run the lighter brainstorming skill instead. Same principle, less ceremony: refine the rough idea through questions before anything gets built.
The reason is simple: a coding agent will build the wrong thing beautifully. It doesn’t push back unless you make pushing back the whole job. Grilling forces me to say out loud what I actually want, exposes the holes in the ticket while they’re still cheap to fix, and leaves me with a plan I can review as an artifact before it turns into code.
Thinking is a separate, explicit phase. That is the single biggest difference between how I worked a year ago and how I work now.
2. Handoff files beat the context window
/clear is my most-used slash command by a wide margin, and it’s usually paired with a handoff file. Together they’re my answer to the context window.
Long sessions degrade. The model starts dragging half-forgotten decisions and dead ends along with it. So instead of fighting that, I treat context as a budget: finish the thinking, dump the plan into a handoff file, clear the session, and let a fresh session pick up the file with nothing else in its head.
Handoffs became connective tissue for more than context management. They hand work to parallel sessions, and they double as ticket descriptions when I split a feature into subtasks. Write the plan once, reuse it everywhere.
3. I orchestrate more than I code
Across those conversations, my sessions spawned 870 subagents. My logs are full of coordination messages between an implementer, a reviewer, and a final reviewer per stack, running concurrently. In the same period the agents executed over 17,000 shell commands and made over 3,300 file edits.
I did very little of the typing. My prompts are about slicing work into stacks, defining what each agent owns, reviewing what comes back, and feeding review comments to the right place. It’s the job of a team lead, except the team writes code faster than any team I’ve ever led, and never gets tired of being told to redo something.
The skill that matters most in this setup isn’t writing code. It’s decomposition: cutting a feature into pieces small enough that an agent can build one piece test-first without stepping on another agent’s work.
4. Small PRs, stacked, always
This is my number one rule, and the data shows I even push back on my own plans: “Can we split up the PRs even more? PR2 seems quite big.”
The logic: when agents generate the code, review becomes the bottleneck and the actual job. And review quality collapses as PR size grows. A 200-line PR gets read. A 2,000-line PR gets skimmed and approved. So a feature becomes a stack: docs first, then schema and migration, then the spec update, then the API, each PR building on the one below it, each one small enough to genuinely review.
Stacking also keeps things moving. Two stacks can progress in parallel in separate worktrees off main, and nothing waits on a merge to keep going.
Where the time actually goes
Here’s the part I would not have told you, because I didn’t know it.
After all that discipline up front, the single biggest recurring activity in my logs is the cleanup loop after a PR opens. Bot reviewers leave comments: CodeRabbit, the linter, the commit linter. I feed each one back to an agent: check if it’s relevant, fix it if it is, respond either way. And every time main
moves, the whole stack needs care: rebase each PR, regenerate migrations and API specs, keep everything green.
The disciplined pipeline didn’t remove maintenance. It moved it to the end and made it predictable. I can live with that trade. But my story said my time went to planning and architecture. The data says a large share of it goes to review janitor work. Worth knowing.
Credit where it’s due
Most of these skills are not mine, and the people who wrote them deserve the mention.
The grilling skills and the handoff pattern come from Matt Pocock’s Skills for Real Engineers, which he published straight from his own agents directory. His framing of the core problem stuck with me: the most common failure mode in AI-assisted development is misalignment, and the fix is making the agent interrogate you before it builds.
Brainstorming, subagent-driven development, TDD, plan writing and the worktree discipline come from Jesse Vincent’s, a full software development methodology packaged as composable skills.
My contribution is mostly the glue: wiring them into the loop above and filling the gaps with a few internal skills we built at work. Both repos are MIT licensed and take minutes to install. If any part of this workflow appeals to you, start there.
What I’d take from this
If you work with coding agents daily, three things:
Measure yourself. Export your history and look at it. Your workflow story and your actual workflow are probably not the same thing, and the gap is where the improvement lives.
Make thinking a separate phase. Grill the spec, write the plan, then build. Every hour of interrogation up front saves a day of building the wrong thing well.
Accept the new job description. With agents writing the code, your value is in slicing work, reviewing output, and keeping the pipeline honest. That’s not a smaller job than writing code. It’s a different one, and most of us were never trained for it.
The one-sentence summary my analysis spat out: I treat myself as an orchestrator running a spec-first, TDD-backed pipeline that turns each ticket into a stack of small, individually reviewed PRs.
I just didn’t know that’s what I was doing until I counted.
